comments: This manuscript describes the construction of a simple knowledge-based expert system (Sanjeevani) using the ESTA/Visual Prolog shell to present ‘‘natural’’ treatment options for diabetes (massage, acupuncture, herbal/nutrition, gems). The write-up is descriptive and procedural but lacks technical depth: there are no concrete examples of rules, no formal description of the knowledge representation, no screenshots or excerpts of the knowledge base, and the referenced figures are not present in the supplied text. There is also no evaluation — no usability study, no validation by clinical experts, no comparison with existing decision-support systems, and no discussion of potential risks or ethical/clinical limitations of providing treatment advice for a medical condition. The literature context is minimal and there is essentially no meaningful comparison to prior work. Writing is repetitive in places and the paper repeats the abstract in the conclusion. Strengths: the topic (medical expert systems) is appropriate for AI/health informatics and the implementation in an existing shell is straightforward. However, as presented the contribution is primarily an engineering demonstration with limited novelty and insufficient evidence that the system is useful or safe. To improve the manuscript the authors should (at minimum) include worked examples or rule excerpts, detailed knowledge-acquisition methodology, expert validation or user testing, a comparison to related systems or approaches, discussion of limitations and clinical safety, and proper citation of prior work. With substantial revision and added validation the work could be of interest to practitioners; in its current form it is not suitable for publication as a research contribution.
